creativeNotes: This is genuinely strong work — the branching structure is sophisticated, the dialogue voice is sharp and age-appropriate, and the central moral pressure point (scene-6/7) is exactly the kind of low-stakes-but-high-meaning choice this age group lives inside every day. Wren's internal voice ('Cool. Love it here already.' / 'I contain multitudes.') is funny without being try-hard, and the choice to let the 'side with Mia' path exist without narrative punishment (per the educational goals) is a mature, non-preachy design decision. The bridge-building path is the standout creative idea in the season so far — it gives players a third way to be brave that isn't just 'confrontation' or 'avoidance.' However, there is one concrete world-consistency problem that needs to be fixed before this ships: the world bible describes 'a contemporary middle school setting,' the target age is 11-14, and yet this episode is explicitly set at 'Ridgemont High' with characters as 'a junior.' That's not a nitpick — it's a structural mismatch between the story world and the bible that will confuse continuity going forward (are these 14-year-old freshmen? Are 'juniors' 16-17-year-olds mentoring an 11-year-old target player?). This needs an explicit decision and a pass to align terminology (school name, grade levels, 'buddy' framing) with the middle school setting, or the world bible needs to be updated to explicitly cover high school — but it can't be silently ambiguous.

story: World mismatch: the world description states 'contemporary middle school setting' but this episode is set at 'Ridgemont High' with Jordan as 'a junior.' This needs to be resolved — either rename to a middle school (e.g., 'Ridgemont Middle') and adjust grade references, or confirm with the showrunner that Season 1 is intentionally high school and update the world bible. This is the one must-fix item.
The convergence structure (hallway -> classroom -> group project -> lunch -> reflection) is well-paced and each choice compounds logically into the next — no notes on structure itself.
Consider a one-line callback in the reflection scene acknowledging Jordan's earlier 'handle sticks' prank — small continuity texture, not required.
character: Alex is drawn with real specificity (the sketchbook, the two-years-and-still-no-table detail) rather than as a generic 'sad kid' — this is a good example of the world's authenticity theme.
Mia toes the line between 'mean girl' cliché and a more textured character (the unguarded 'don't tell anyone today was a good day' line in 9b) — good work resisting the flat antagonist trap.
Jordan is consistent and charming throughout without stealing focus from the player's choices — good use of a low-stakes secondary character as an emotional barometer.
dialogue: Player VO like 'Great plan, Wren. Really airtight' and 'Cool. Love it here already' land as authentically teenage — dry, deflective, self-aware. No notes.

Alex's 'Apparently you need to talk to people. Rude, honestly' is a great character-voice moment — dry humor emerging from someone established as withdrawn feels earned rather than tonally inconsistent.
tone: The lunchroom cruelty beat (scene-6/7) is handled with restraint — Mia's mockery is 'quiet cruelty' as promised in the synopsis, not cartoonish villainy, which fits the theme of belonging without moralizing.
Good instinct not to narratively punish the 'side with Mia' path — it stays uncomfortable (7c) rather than melodramatic, which respects the audience's intelligence.

inspirationReferences.title: Eighth Grade
inspirationReferences.reasoning: Similar commitment to small, specific social stakes (seating, lunch tables) carrying real emotional weight without adult melodrama.

inspirationReferences.title: Life is Strange
inspirationReferences.reasoning: Comparable use of branching micro-choices that shape reputation/identity over time rather than plot-mechanical outcomes.
